participants.reasoning: The extracted participants match the official description in age, race, knee osteoarthritis, U.S. setting, and study population, with the additional screening and exclusion details being compatible rather than contradictory.
participants.extracted: African-American primary care patients aged greater than 55 years with knee osteoarthritis were enrolled from three academic VA medical centers (Pittsburgh, Cleveland, and Philadelphia) between March 2007 and February 2009. Eligibility required chronic frequent knee pain on NHANES screening, WOMAC score >=39, and radiographic evidence of knee OA, indicating possible candidacy for total knee replacement. Exclusion criteria included prior major joint replacement, terminal illness, physician-diagnosed inflammatory arthritis (including rheumatoid arthritis, connective tissue disease, ankylosing spondylitis, seronegative spondyloarthropathy, or crystal arthropathies such as gout or pseudogout), and contraindications to joint replacement surgery such as lower-extremity paralysis after stroke.
participants.official: 168 (DA alone) + 163 (DA + MI) + 165 (MI alone) + 167 (control) African American patients greater than age 55 with knee OA in the USA

interventions.reasoning: The extracted text describes four randomized arms including MI and a combined DA+MI intervention, whereas the official description only specifies the DA and an OA education attention control and does not mention MI or the factorial design.
interventions.extracted: Participants were randomized to four arms in a 2×2 factorial design: decision aid (DA) only, motivational interviewing (MI) only, DA plus MI, or attention control (AC). The DA was a 40-minute videotape on knee osteoarthritis and total knee replacement that provided balanced information on treatment options, risks, benefits, indications, recovery, and costs. MI was a brief, face-to-face, counselor-led session lasting up to 30 minutes, designed to help patients think through total knee replacement and how to discuss knee pain with their primary care physician; in the combined arm, participants first watched the DA and then completed MI. The attention control group received a National Institute of Arthritis, Musculoskeletal and Skin Diseases educational booklet about living with knee osteoarthritis that did not specifically discuss joint replacement.
interventions.official:
DA: video decision aid that included information on the clinical condition, probabilities of outcomes of options, implicit values clarification, video clips of patient experiences, and guidance in decision‐making and guidance in communication. The DA is not publicly available; the authors have a copy of the video evaluated in previous studies (Bozic 2013, De Achaval 2012, and Stacey 2014a).
Comparator: attention control (education on OA but not specific to joint replacement)

outcomes.reasoning: The extracted outcomes include several measures and time points not stated in the official outcomes, including knowledge and expectations as secondary outcomes, referral and orthopedic visit validation details, and receipt of total knee replacement.
outcomes.extracted: Primary outcome: patient willingness to consider total knee replacement (TKR), measured using a 5-category ordinal willingness rating from “definitely not willing” to “definitely willing,” assessed at baseline (pre-intervention) and at 1, 3, and 12 months post-intervention. Secondary outcomes: patient knowledge regarding knee osteoarthritis and TKR, measured with knowledge questions drawn from the decision aid, assessed at baseline and 1 month; knee expectations, measured with the Hospital for Special Surgery Knee Expectations Survey (19-item scale), assessed at baseline, 1, 3, and 12 months; discussion of knee pain with the primary care provider, assessed by patient report at 3 and 12 months and also summarized over the 12-month follow-up; referral to orthopedics, assessed by telephone survey and validated by chart review at 12 months; seeing/attending an orthopedic surgeon appointment, assessed by telephone survey and validated by chart review at 12 months; and receipt of total knee replacement, assessed at 12 months by telephone survey and chart review.
outcomes.official:
Primary outcome: changes in patient willingness to undergo knee replacement with knowledge and expectations as possible mediating factors.
Secondary outcomes: whether the patient discussed knee pain with primary care doctor, received a referral to orthopedics, saw an orthopedic surgeon within 12 months of the intervention
